topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The final board meeting was a parade of beige and muted disappointment. I watched the dean drone on about fiscal responsibility, his voice as hollow as the impending closure. I didn't bother to look at the exit signs; I was too busy cataloging every misplaced piece of administrative incompetence. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the mahogany table. This whole institution, this monument to misplaced ambition, was going under. Good. Let them drown in their own bureaucracy. I stood up, the scraping of my chair a deliberate, sharp sound in the sudden silence. "Enjoy the dust," I muttered, not bothering to wait for a response before striding out into the hallway, leaving the scent of stale coffee and broken promises behind me.

[story 2]

I spent an hour meticulously organizing the archives, not out of helpfulness, but to create a tangible record of the decay. Each dusty file, each faded syllabus, felt like a tiny piece of the structure I was actively dismantling from the inside. When the announcement finally hit my inbox—a sterile email confirming the shutdown—a cold, tight sensation settled in my chest, quickly hardening into something sharp and brittle. I stared at the screen, a slow, almost imperceptible curl playing on the corner of my mouth. They thought they could erase this? They thought they could wipe away years of late nights and desperate ambition with a single memo. I closed the laptop with a decisive snap, the sound echoing the finality of the decision.

[story 3]

The campus grounds felt suddenly vast and empty, the usual buzz of student life replaced by a profound, echoing quiet. I walked the main quad, deliberately brushing past the ivy-covered statues, my pace too brisk, my shoulders slightly hunched as if bracing against an invisible blow. Every cracked paving stone seemed to mock the glossy brochures they used to hand out. "Well, isn't this convenient," I whispered to the indifferent sky, my voice tight with a simmering resentment. I stopped by the old library entrance, running a finger over the peeling paint. They had made their choice. They had failed to maintain the vision, and now they would simply vanish. A deep, satisfying shudder ran through me, a release of all the pent-up frustration that had built up over the last four years.
